title: What Energy Is
paragraphs: At the most fundamental level, energy is the capacity to do work — to move matter, to change temperature, to drive chemical reactions, to transmit information. The laws of thermodynamics govern everything: energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another (the first law), and every conversion involves some irreversible loss to entropy, typically as waste heat (the second law). These are not abstractions. They are the hard physical constraints that every power station, every battery, every engine, and every grid must operate within. The efficiency of any energy system is ultimately bounded by thermodynamics, and the history of energy engineering is largely the history of pushing closer to those theoretical limits.

Humanity's primary energy sources fall into a remarkably short list. Solar energy — electromagnetic radiation from the Sun — is the original source of almost all energy on Earth. It drives photosynthesis (which produced the biomass that became fossil fuels over geological time), heats the atmosphere (creating the pressure differentials that drive wind), and evaporates water (which falls as rain and flows downhill through hydroelectric turbines). Direct solar capture through photovoltaic cells converts photons into electron flow using the photovoltaic effect in semiconductor junctions. Wind turbines extract kinetic energy from moving air masses, converting it to rotational mechanical energy that drives electrical generators. Hydroelectric dams harness the gravitational potential energy of water at elevation. Geothermal energy taps the heat flowing outward from the Earth's core and mantle. Tidal and wave energy capture the mechanical energy of ocean movements driven by lunar and solar gravity.

Nuclear energy operates on entirely different physics. Nuclear fission splits the nuclei of heavy elements — primarily uranium-235 — releasing energy from the strong nuclear force that binds protons and neutrons together. The energy density is extraordinary: one kilogram of natural uranium, fully utilised in a breeder reactor, contains roughly 80,000 times the energy of one kilogram of coal. Nuclear fusion, the process that powers the Sun, combines light nuclei (isotopes of hydrogen) under extreme temperature and pressure to release even more energy per unit mass. Fossil fuels — coal, petroleum, and natural gas — store chemical energy in hydrocarbon bonds formed from ancient biological matter compressed and heated over millions of years. Burning them reverses that storage, releasing energy and carbon dioxide.

Electricity is the dominant form of secondary energy — a carrier, not a source. Roughly 40% of global primary energy is converted to electricity, a share that is rising as transport, heating, and industrial processes electrify. The physics of electricity generation is remarkably consistent across technologies: nearly all methods (except photovoltaics and fuel cells) ultimately drive a rotating turbine connected to an electromagnetic generator. Coal burns to heat water to steam that spins a turbine. Gas turbines combust natural gas directly. Nuclear reactors heat water to steam. Wind turns rotor blades. Hydro sends water through turbine runners. Even geothermal and concentrated solar power use heat to create steam. The generator itself works on Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction — rotating a conductor through a magnetic field induces an electrical current.

The electrical grid is the infrastructure that connects generation to consumption. It operates at multiple voltage levels: generation typically produces at 11-25 kV, step-up transformers boost this to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission (higher voltage means lower current and lower resistive losses over distance), and step-down transformers reduce it to 240V (in Australia) or 120V (in North America) for household use. The grid must balance supply and demand instantaneously — any mismatch causes frequency deviations that can damage equipment or trigger cascading failures. This real-time balancing act is one of the most complex engineering challenges in modern infrastructure, and it is becoming more complex as variable renewable sources replace dispatchable fossil generation.

Energy density — the amount of energy stored per unit mass or volume — is one of the most important practical metrics in energy engineering. Diesel fuel contains roughly 45 megajoules per kilogram. Lithium-ion batteries store about 0.5-1.0 MJ/kg. Hydrogen gas at standard pressure holds 142 MJ/kg by mass but only 0.01 MJ per litre by volume, which is why compression or liquefaction is required for practical storage and transport. Uranium-235 contains approximately 82,000,000 MJ/kg. These differences are not trivia — they explain why aircraft run on kerosene rather than batteries, why electric cars need fast-charging networks rather than five-minute refuelling, and why nuclear submarines can operate for decades without refuelling.
keyPoints: Energy is governed by the laws of thermodynamics — it cannot be created or destroyed, and every conversion loses some energy to entropy as waste heat.
Nearly all electricity generation (except solar PV and fuel cells) works by spinning a turbine connected to an electromagnetic generator, regardless of the primary energy source.
Solar radiation is the ultimate source of most energy on Earth — driving wind, rain, photosynthesis, and the fossil fuels formed from ancient biomass.
Nuclear energy operates on fundamentally different physics from chemical energy, with energy densities roughly five to six orders of magnitude greater than fossil fuels.
The electrical grid must balance supply and demand in real time, with frequency serving as the physical indicator of that balance — 50 Hz in Australia and Europe, 60 Hz in North America.
Energy density explains most of the practical constraints in energy engineering — why planes burn kerosene, why batteries are heavy, and why hydrogen needs compression.
Electricity is a carrier, not a source — roughly 40% of global primary energy is currently converted to electricity, a share that is rising with electrification.
Geothermal and tidal energy are the only major sources not ultimately derived from solar radiation or nuclear reactions within human fuel cycles.

furtherReading.title: Sustainable Energy — Without the Hot Air
furtherReading.author: David J.C. MacKay
furtherReading.description: A rigorous, quantitative assessment of whether renewable energy can meet the UK's energy needs, using straightforward arithmetic that applies to any country. Free to read online.

furtherReading.title: Energy: A Human History
furtherReading.author: Richard Rhodes
furtherReading.description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning author traces four centuries of energy transitions — from wood to coal to oil to nuclear — revealing the human stories behind each transformation.

furtherReading.title: The Grid: The Fraying Wires Between Americans and Our Energy Future
furtherReading.author: Gretchen Bakke
furtherReading.description: An accessible exploration of how the electrical grid actually works, why it is struggling to adapt to modern demands, and what the future of grid infrastructure looks like.

furtherReading.title: IEA World Energy Outlook (annual)
furtherReading.author: International Energy Agency
furtherReading.description: The definitive annual reference for global energy data, projections, and policy analysis across all fuels and technologies.
